coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence studies of double-emitting carbon dots and application in detection of H2O in ethanol and differentiation of cancer cell and normal cell
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Hao
Liao, Xiaokun
Lu, Xingjian
Hu, Xiaoyan
Xiong, Yue
Cao, Jun
Pan, Jiaqi
Li, Chaorong
Zheng, Yingying
coredata.dc:description: Herein, phenylalanine-bound carbon dots (CDs) with dual fluorescence emission peak behavior were reported, one of which was excitation-dependent and the other was not. The amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs had a certain influence on their fluorescence. When the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs were removed, their fluorescence was significantlyweakened. The prepared CDs had solvent effect, and the emission peak of CDs shifted according to the change of the polarity of the solvent. Based on this, a method was developed for detecting water content in ethanol using CDs as a ratio probe. The change in the ratio of the intensity of the two emission peaks corresponded to the change of water content in ethanol. The detection linearity was good. In addition, the resulting CDs had good biocompatibility and could be enormously transported into cancer cells by the amino acid transporter on the surface of cancer cells, while normal cells only had a small amount of CDsthroughendocytosis, which laid the base for the distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells. The unique fluorescence properties of the prepared CDs achieved their applications in both water detection and cell differentiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phenylalanine-boundcarbondots
Dual-emission fluorescence
Selectivefluorescenceimaging
H2O detection
Surface group

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the effect of NH2 and COOH group on the fluorescence of 1,5-CDs
Preparation and structural characterization of CDs
The effect of NH2 and COOH group on the fluorescence of 1,5-CDs
The solvent effect andwater contentdetecting inethanol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51802282) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and HRTEM (inset) image of 1,5-CDs, (b) size distribution of 1,5-CDs, (c) TEM image of 1-CDs, (d) TEM image of 0-CDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of 0-CDs, 1-CDs, and 1,5-CDs and high resolution C1s XPS fitting results for (b) 0-CDs. (c) 1-CDs. (d) 1,5-CDs.
The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths (a) 0-CDs. (b) 1-CDs. (c) 1,5-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (d) 0-CDs. (e) 1-CDs. (f) 1,5-CDs.
The FT-IR spectra of (a) 1,5-CDs and NH2 null 1,5-CDs and (b) 1,5-CDs and COOH null 1,5-CDs.

(a) The FL spectra of 1,5-CDs after adding different total masses of EDC and NHS, excited by 335 nm. (b) The relationship between different total masses of EDC and NHS and the two peak intensity ratios (I2/I1) of 1,5-CDs. (c) The FL spectra of 1,5-CDs after adding different volumes of Hydrazine hydrate, excited by 335 nm. (d) The relationship between different volumes of Hydrazine hydrate and the two peak intensity ratios (I2/I1) of 1,5-CDs. (e) The UV–vis absorption spectra of 1,5-CDs with different masses of EDC and NHS. (f) The UV–vis absorption spectra of 1,5-CDs with different volumes of Hydrazine hydrate.

(a)The FL spectra of the 1,5-CDs excited by 335 nm in different solvents. (b) Normalized emission peaks FL spectra at long wavelengths. (c) PL spectra (Ex = 335 nm) of the 1,5-CDs in ethanol upon the addition of water. (d) The relationship between different percentages of water in ethanol and the two peak intensity ratios of 1,5-CDs.
Viability of MCF-7 and EC cell lines with different concentrations of 1,5-CDs.

Fluorescence images of the (a)EC cell and (b)MCF-7 cell after incubated with 1,5-CDs under the concentration of 100 μg/ml. Confocal laser scanning microscope images of MCF-7 cells after incubation with (c) 0-CDs. (d) 1-CDs. (e) Confocal laser scanning microscope images of MCF-7 cells co-cultured with phenylalanine and then cultured with 1,5-CDs.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of 1,5-CDs and their application in water content detecting and targeted bioimaging of LAT1 overexpressed cancer cell.
Carbon element analysis of XPS spectra of three carbon dots quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence studies of double-emitting carbon dots and application in detection of H2O in ethanol and differentiation of cancer cell and normal cell

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, phenylalanine-bound carbon dots (CDs) with dual fluorescence emission peak behavior were reported, one of which was excitation-dependent and the other was not. The amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs had a certain influence on their fluorescence. When the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs were removed, their fluorescence was significantlyweakened. The prepared CDs had solvent effect, and the emission peak of CDs shifted according to the change of the polarity of the solvent. Based on this, a method was developed for detecting water content in ethanol using CDs as a ratio probe. The change in the ratio of the intensity of the two emission peaks corresponded to the change of water content in ethanol. The detection linearity was good. In addition, the resulting CDs had good biocompatibility and could be enormously transported into cancer cells by the amino acid transporter on the surface of cancer cells, while normal cells only had a small amount of CDsthroughendocytosis, which laid the base for the distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells. The unique fluorescence properties of the prepared CDs achieved their applications in both water detection and cell differentiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth and aging of the global population, the incidence and mortality of cancer are increasing rapidly [1]. Cancer treatment is still a challenge worldwide, so early diagnosis is very important for cancer treatment. Compared with healthy cells, cancer cells have some unique biomarkers, such as: CD44 [2,3], folate receptor [4], cathepsin B [5], etc. These biomarkers play an important role in the early diagnosis of cancer. Metabolic reprogramming is also a hallmark of cancer [6], and one aspect of metabolic reprogramming observed in cancer cells is dysregulated amino acid uptake, the entry of amino acids into cancer cells via membrane transporters. The L-type amino acid transporter 1 (LAT1) is an Na+ independent neutral amino acid transporter, which was found to be abnormally expressed in tumor cells to meet the needs of tumor proliferation [7,8]. LAT1 preferentially transports large neutral amino acids such as phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan, histidine, etc [9]. Therefore, targeting cancer cells with phenylalanine ligand-binding CDs is a better way to differentiate cancer cells.

CDs are fluorescent carbon nanoparticles around 10 nm in size [10,11]. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CDs have good photostability, low biotoxicity, and high sensitivity, which have attracted much attention in the fields of cell imaging [12–16], chemical sensing [17–23], and photocatalysis [24–27]. The unique photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs can be altered by changing the synthesis parameters [28], the nature of the reactants and their concentrations, the incorporation of various heteroatoms, etc [29,30]. Because the factors affecting the fluorescence emission of CDs are too complex, the fluorescence mechanism is not very clear, and some studies have shown that functional groups are one of the emission sources [31–33].

Most of the reported CDs have single-emission peaks [34–36], and there are not many reports of double-emission CDs. In the past few years, researchers have begun to pay attention to the dual-emission characteristics of CDs. At present, double-emitting CDs are mostly used as fluorescent ratio probes to detect various substances [37–42], because they greatly reduce the interference of factors such as concentration, temperature, solvent polarity, and pH value of the environment, making the results more accurate. For example, Qing wen Guan et al used a one-step solvothermal method to synthesize double-emitting nitrogen-sulfur co-doped CDs for quantitative detection of water content in ethanol [43].

In this work, we obtained phenylalanine-bound CDs by a one-step solvothermal method and filtration and dialysis purification. The obtained CDs had two fluorescence emission peaks under one excitation, one of which was excitation-dependent, and the other was invariant with the change of excitation wavelength. We also explored the effects of solvent polarity and the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs on their fluorescence. And the double emission characteristic of CDs was utilized to detect water in ethanol. In addition, the synthesized phenylalanine-bound CDs could distinguish cancer cells from normal cells due to the more abundant LAT1 in MCF-7 cancer cells than in epithelial cells (EC). The specific steps were shown in Scheme 1 .

Anthraquinone, 1-Aminoanthraquinone, 1, 5-Diaminoanthraquinone, Hydrazine hydratewere, Tetrahydrofuran(THF) and Phenylalanine were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (China). Citric acid (CA), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3 - (3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride) (EDC) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (China). Ethanol, Ethylene Glycol(EG), Ethyl acetate(EtOAc) and Isopropyl alcohol(IPA) were obtained from Hangzhou Gao Jing Chemical (China). Toluene and Acetone were purchased from Zhejiang San Ying Chemical Reagent Co.Ltd (China). Phosphate buffered solution (PBS) and Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) were obtained from Hyclone (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and penicillin–streptomycin were purchased from Gibco Co. Ltd (USA).

The microscopic appearance and lattice fringes of CDs were transmitted by transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEM-2100). The phase analysis of CDs was performed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD DX-2700B). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained by Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer. The PL spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by a Nicolet FT-IR 5700 spectrometer. Elemental species and chemical bonds of carbon dots were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements (Kratos Axis Ultra system). Fluorescence imagings were obtained by confocal laser scanning microscope (FV100-IX81). Toxicity was detected by microplate reader ELx800.

In this paper, phenylalanine-bound double-emitting CDs were prepared by solvothermal method. The phenylalanine(0.07 g), 1,5-diaminoanthraquinone(0.025 g) and citric acid(0.025 g) were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol, and the mixture was then added into a 25 mL Teflon reactor, sealed and heated at 190 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter. The solution was then added to a cellulose ester dialysis bag (MWCO: 3500 Da). The phenylalanine-bound CDs were gradually precipitated from deionized water [44], and dialyzed in deionized water for a total of 48 h. During dialysis, deionized water was changed every 12 h. Powder samples were collected by freeze-drying after dialysis. The prepared carbon dotsisdesignatedas 1,5-CDs on account of the raw material 1,5-diaminoanthraquinone.

For comparing the effect of the number of amino groups in the raw materials on the nitrogen content in the prepared carbon dots, the raw material 1, 5-diaminoanthraquinone (with two amino groups) was replaced with1-aminoanthraquinone (with one amino groups) or anthraquinone (without amino group), and the carbon dots were synthesized by the same method above and named as 1-CDs and 0-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the effect of NH2 and COOH group on the fluorescence of 1,5-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Different total mass (0.4 mg-8 mg) of NHS and EDC (themolarratio of NHS and EDC was 1:1) were add to 2 mL 0.5 mg/ml 1,5-CDs solution and the mixed solution was stirred in the dark for 3 h to amidate the amino groups on the surface of 1,5-CDs, thereby reducing the number of amino groups. After that, theirfluorescenceintensity and UV weremeasured and the effect of the mass (0.4 mg-8 mg) of NHS and EDC on the fluorescence performance and UV were studied. Dialysis (MWCO: 3500 Da) with water was then performed to purify the CDs, followed by freeze-drying to remove excess solvent and get the powder. FT-IR test was then taken for the powders.

In order to reduce the number of carboxyl groups on the surface of 1,5-CDs, different volumes (50 μL–300 μL) of hydrazine hydrate were added to 3 mL 0.5 mg/ml 1,5-CDs solution, and the mixed solution was also stirred for 3 h in the dark. After that, theirfluorescenceintensity and UV weremeasured and the effect of the volumes (50 μL–300 μL) of hydrazine hydrate on the fluorescence performance and UV were studied. Dialysis (MWCO: 3500 Da) with water was then performed to purify the CDs, followed by freeze-drying to remove excess solvent and get the powder. FT-IR test was then taken for the powders.
1 mg 1,5-CDs was dissolved in 3 mL of THF, Toluene, Acetone, EG, Ethyl Acetate (EtOAc) and IPA, respectively. After that, the fluorescence spectrum was tested at the excitation wavelength of 335 nm.

0.2 g 1,5-CDs were dissolved in 20 mL ethanol to get the solution. Thepipettewas usedtotransfer 0.4 mL 1,5-CDs-dissolved ethanol solution into1.6 mL water to prepare a proportional 80% (byvolume) water–ethanol solution. In the same way, we prepared different proportional solutions of 10%–70% (byvolume). Finally, the fluorescence of the solutions was detected under excitation at 335 nm.

The cytotoxicity of 1,5-CDs was assessed by the CCK-8 method. The EC cell suspension and MCF-7 cell suspension were cultured on a 96-well plate (100 μL cells per well), and counted using a hemocytometer, and the estimated cell density was about 1 × 104 cells/well. The cells were then cultured with DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% (v/v) penicillin/streptomycin in a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h. A series of 1,5-CDs solutions with different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, 100 μg/mL) were added to each well respectively, and then were placed in a cell incubator for co-cultivation for 24 h. Cells without 1,5-CDs were used as a control group. Three parallel groups were set each time. Then, CCK-8 (10 μL) was added to each well to treat cells in a dark environment, and the 96-well plate was placed in a cell culture incubator for 2 h. Finally, absorbance (A) of each well was measured at a wavelength of 450 nm with a microplate reader. Cell viability was calculated according to the following formula:
Cell
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
A
treat
/
A
control
× 100

There, Atreat was the absorbance of the sample when cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and treated with 1,5-CDs solutions, and Acontrol was the absorbance of the sample containing cells and growth medium (DMEM). The numbers of experimental group, blank group and control group were three, respectively, n = 3).

EC cells and MCF-7 cells at a cell density of 1 × 105/mL in DMEM medium were cultured in 20 mm dishes at 37 °C in a cell incubator containing 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, the original medium was replaced with 1 mL of CDs with a concentration of 100 μg/mL plus 1 mL of DMEM medium, and cultured in a cell incubator with the same environment for two hours. After that, the supernatant was removed and the cells were washed three times with 1 mL PBS for 1 min each time. Finally, 1 mL of PBS was added for imaging under a laser confocal microscope.

To study the influence of other LAT1 binder, the phenylalanine aqueous solution was first co-cultured with MCF-7 cells for 5 h, then the phenylalanine aqueous solution was taken out, and the CDs solution was then added to incubate for 2 h. Cell imagings were taken under a laser confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structural characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Phenylalanine-bound CDs (1,5-CDs, 1-CDs and 0-CDs) were prepared by solvothermalsynthesismethod at 190 °C for 5 h using phenylalanine, 1,5-diaminoanthraquinone (1-aminoanthraquinone or anthraquinone) and citric acid as raw materials and ethanol as solvent. After dialysis to get rid of the unreacted raw materials and solvent, a red solid powder was obtained by freeze-drying. According to Mr. Ji’s new research [45], we speculated that the formation mechanism of CDs may be due to the dehydration and condensation reaction of anthraquinone and phenylalanine under acidic conditions to form a rigid carbon skeleton. Subsequently, the skeleton was combined to form high N-doped CDs with large rigid π conjugate structure, and carboxyl and amino groups were formed at the edge. In addition to providing acidic conditions, citric acid also acted as a precursor and participated in the formation of CDs.

Three CDs were observed using a TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b, the 1,5-CDs appeareduniformsphericalshape with good dispersibility and a diameter distribution of 2–5 nm and an average diameter of 3.02 nm. The lattice fringe spacing of 1,5-CDs was 0.24 nm, which corresponded to the (1120) plane of graphene [4]. Fig. 1c and Fig. 1d showed that the morphological features of 0-CDs and 1-CDs were also uniform spherical and uniformly dispersed. At the same time, the XRD of as-synthesized CDs (0-CDs, 1-CDs and 1,5-CDs) (Fig. S1) had a diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.8°, which could be indexed to the (002) plane of graphene. The XRD pattern indicated that CDs formed graphene structure.

The functional groups on CDs were determined using FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the absorption peak located at 3428 cm−1 could be attributed to the O-H stretching vibration. The peak at 3310 cm−1 was assigned to the N-H stretching vibration. The peaks centered at 2932 cm−1, 1706 cm−1, 1611 cm−1, 1386 cm−1,1157 cm−1 were corresponded to the stretching vibration of C-H, CO, CN, C-N, C-O, respectively, which suggested the formation of polyaromatic structures and the presence of free carboxyl and amino groups at the edge of 1-CDs and 1,5-CDs.While, the peak at 1386 cm−1 (C-N) was accompanied by reduced intensity and the peak at 3310 cm−1 (N-H) almost disappeared in 0-CDs, showing there was no amino groups of 0-CDs. Meanwhile, the C-N bond strength at 1386 cm−1 of 1,5-CDs was stronger than that of 1-CDs. It could be seen from Fig. 2a (magnificationofonesegmentofthespectrum) that the C-N bond strength of 1,5-CDs was almost equal to that of the bond next to it (1460 cm−1), while the C-N bond strength of 1-CDs was relatively much smaller. This showed that 1,5-CDs had more amino groups than 1-CDs.

XPS spectra could know the types and contents of surface elements and the types of chemical bonds of the three CDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of 0-CDs (Fig. 2b) had three peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.7 eV, which could be attributed to CC/C-C, C-N, C-O-C, respectively, while 1-CDs (Fig. 2c) and 1,5-CDs (Fig. 2d) had three peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV, 289.7 eV, ascribed to CC/C-C, C-N, N-CO, respectively. Both 1-CDs and 1,5-CDs had amide bonds (N-CO), but 0-CDs did not. This was because there was no amino group on the surface of anthraquinone, so it could not combine with the carboxyl group of phenylalanine to form an amide bond.
It could be seen from Table 1 that with the increase of the number of amino groups on the surface of anthraquinone, the relative content of C-N bonds of the synthesized carbon dots also increased.

UV and PL could be used to characterize the optical properties of the three CDs. 0-CDs were dissolved in ethanol and a yellow solution appeared, and it emitted blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. Anthraquinone, the raw material for preparing 0-CDs, had no amide groups on its surface and could not be combined with phenylalanine. The fluorescence emission of 0-CDs had only one emission peak, and the optimal emission peak was located at 460 nm (Fig. 3 a). Its fluorescence emission peak was found to be excitation-dependent. The solution of 1-CDs and 1,5-CDs in ethanol emitted orange and yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, respectively. 1-CDs and 1,5-CDs had two fluorescence emission peaks. Their first emission peak was excitation-dependent, while the second emission peak was not excitation-dependent. The first optimal emission peak of 1-CDs was located at 412 nm, and the second optimal emission peak was located at 564 nm (Fig. 3b). The first optimal emission peak of 1,5-CDs was located at 363 nm, and the second optimal emission peak was located at 563 nm (Fig. 3c). It could be seen from Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c that the intensity ratio of emission peaks at long wavelengths to short wavelengths for 1-CDs was greater than that for 1,5-CDs, which might be due to the less numbers of phenylalanine on the surface of 1-CDs, resulting in a lower emission peak intensity at its short wavelength. 0-CDs had UV absorption peak at 374.5 nm (Fig. 3d), 1-CDs had UV absorption peaks at 308 nm and 475 nm (Fig. 3e), and 1,5-CDs had UV absorption peaks at 275 nm, 299 nm and 487 nm (Fig. 3f). The absorption band below 300 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated C = C bond of the carbon core, while the absorption band above 300 nm was attributed to the n-π* jump of the carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface of the CDs [46,47]. In order to test the fluorescence stability of 1,5-CDs, a 30-minute xenon lamp irradiation experiment on 1,5-CDs was conducted and the fluorescence intensity of 1,5-CDs was tested every 5 min. As could be seen from the Fig. S2, after the irradiation of xenon lamp, the fluorescence intensity of 1,5-CDs had hardly changed, showing a good photostability goodstabilityagainstphotobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of NH2 and COOH group on the fluorescence of 1,5-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Surface chemistry affected the electronic configuration of 1,5-CDs and thus their photoluminescent properties.[48] Here both NH2 and COOH groups on the surface of 1,5-CDs were studied. First, different total mass (0.4–8.0 mg) of NHS and EDC were added to the 1,5-CDs solution, and the mixed solution was stirred in the dark for 3 h to amidate the amino groups on the surface of 1,5-CDs, thereby reducing the number of amino groups. Dialysis was then performed, followed by freeze-drying to remove excess solvent.

According to the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 4 a), it could be seen that the change before and after the reaction was very large. The peaks of 3309 cm−1 and 859 cm−1 corresponding to the N-H stretching vibration and bending vibration respectively were reduced. The intensity of the amino peak after the reaction (NH2 null 1,5-CDs) was significantly lower than that of the hydroxyl peak, which indicated a decrease in the number of amino groups.

Different volumes (50–300 μL) of hydrazine hydrate were added to the 1,5-CDs solution, and also stirred for 3 h in the dark to reduce the number of carboxyl groups. Dialysis and freeze drying were also performed [49]. From FT-IR spectra (Fig. 4b), the relative intensity ratio of the peaks at 3428 cm−1 (O-H) and 3315 cm−1 (N-H) decreased after the addition of hydrazine hydrate, indicating the formation of amide. And after the reaction (COOH null 1,5-CDs), the relative peak intensity of the carbonyl group to amide group also decreased significantly. The above indicated that carboxyl groups in CDs became amide group as shown in the Fig. 4b.

The intensities of the fluorescence emission of 1,5-CDs after the reaction with NHS and EDC or hydrazine hydrate were detected respectively. As shown in the Fig. 5 a, the intensities of the two fluorescence emission peaks of 1,5-CDs both decreased with the decrease of the number of amino groups. The intensity ratio of the second emission peak (I2, 563 nm) to the first emission peak (I1, 363 nm) were taken as the ordinate, the quantity of the added NHS and EDC (0–8.0 mg) was taken as the abscissa, and the linear fitting was performed. As shown in the Fig. 5b, it had a good linear relationship and the standard linear correlation coefficient R2 was 0.99602. The Fig. 5c showed that the intensities of the two fluorescence emission peaks of 1,5-CDs also decreased with the decrease of the number of carboxyl groups due to the adding of hydrazine hydrate. A linear fit plot was got in the same way and the standard linear correlation coefficient R2 was 0.96747 in the Fig. 5d. It showed that the effect of surface groups on the fluorescence of CDs was linearly correlated. It could be found from the two linear relationship diagrams that as the number of surface amino groups and carboxyl groups decreased, I2/I1 increased, that was to say, the intensity of the first emission peak decreased as the number of surface groups decreased, indicating that the effect of emission peaks was positively correlated. The above data showed that both carboxyl and amino groups affected the fluorescence intensities of the two emission peaks of CDs, but the first emission peak was more affected than the second emission peak.

Their UV was measured again (Fig. 5e and f), and the reduction of carboxyl group had little effect on the UV of 1,5-CDs, while the reduction of amino group made the peak of 1,5-CDs below 400 nm red-shifted and the intensity also increased, whereas the peak at 480 nm was only a little change in intensity. This might be when the amide bond was connected to a benzene ring or a double bond, because the conjugation effect of electron-donating caused by the lone pair of electrons of N was stronger than the inductive effect of electron-withdrawing (the electronegativity of N was stronger than that of C), the amide group was an electron-donating group, and the conjugation system increased and the degree of conjugation increased, so it led to a red shift.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solvent effect andwater contentdetecting inethanol
ce:section.ce:para: The solvent effect of 1,5-CDs were also studied. 1,5-CDs was dissolved in THF, Toluene, Acetone, EG, Ethyl Acetate and IPA, respectively. It was found that 1,5-CDs were soluble in these solvents, and the characteristics of the fluorescence emission peaks were similar to those in ethanol. There were two emission peaks, and one was excitation-dependent and the other was not. We put together the fluorescence curves of 1,5-CDs in six solvents at the excitation wavelength of 335 nm (Fig. 6 a) and found that both the first and second emission peaks were red-shifted compared with in ethanol. We normalized the curve of the second emission peak (Fig. 6b) and found that the second emission peak was red-shifted from 535 nm to 580 nm with increasing solvent polarity. This was similar to previous reports [50–52]. The solvent effect was usually caused by the combination of the solvent and the defects or groups on the surface of the 1,5-CDs, which was related to the polarity of the solvent, the size of the hydrogen bond between the solvent and the 1,5-CDs, and the effect of the dipole–dipole interaction on the surface electrons of the 1,5-CDs.

The 1,5-CDs were used to detect the water content in ethanol[43,53,54]. 1,5-CDs were first dissolved in ethanol, and then a pipette was used to take 0.4 mL of carbon dot ethanol solution and 1.6 mL of deionized water to prepare a 80% water content ethanol solution, and the same method to prepare 10%-80% water–ethanol solution. It could be visualized from Fig. 6c that the fluorescence of 1,5-CDs was gradually quenched with increasing water content. The fluorescence intensity ratio of the two emission peaks (I2/I1) had a good linear relationship with the water content in ethanol, and the R2 was 0.98686 (Fig. 6d). In this experiment, the mechanism of CDs sensing might be the aggregation induced quenching effect. The increase of water content led to the change of solvent polarity, which caused the aggregation of CDs, and then led to fluorescence quenching. The content of water in the organic solvent could be detected by this method [54].

MCF-7 cancer cells and EC normal cell were used to measure the cytotoxicity of 1,5-CDs by CCK-8 method. The absorbance was measured at 450 nm by a microplate reader. The obtained data was demonstrated in Fig. 7 . The cell viability of the five concentrations of 1,5-CDs was calculated to be 99.41%, 99.46%, 98.53%, 94.22%, and 91.33%, respectively. These were all above the 70% cytotoxicity criterion. It showed that the prepared 1,5-CDs could be used for cancer diagnosis materials.

The application of the prepared nanomaterials in cancer cell distinguishing was demonstrated by incubating MCF-7 cells or EC cells with CDs, 1-CDs and 1,5-CDs. Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of EC cells (Fig. 8 a) showed that the 1,5-CDs emitted yellow fluorescence inside the cells, but only a small amount of 1,5-CDs entered the EC cells, resulting in a low fluorescence intensity. Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of MCF-7 cells (Fig. 8b) showed that MCF-7 cells had stronger fluorescence intensity than EC cells, which indicated that there were more 1,5-CDs in MCF-7 cells. Amino acid transporters located on the plasma membrane mediate amino acid transport across the plasma membrane. The L-type amino acid transporter 1 (LAT1) is an Na+ independent neutral amino acid transporter, which was found to be abnormally expressed in tumor cells [7,8]. The existence of LAT1 is related to the absorption and reabsorption of amino acids by tumor cells, and the overexpression of LAT1 plays an important role in the continuous proliferation of malignant tumor cells. LAT1 preferentially transports large neutral amino acids such as phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan, histidine, etc [55]. In this paper, the prepared 1,5-CDs were phenylalanine-bound carbon dots. 1,5-CDs were firstly bound to overexpressed LAT1 on the surface of MCF-7 cells and were then transported across the plasma membrane and entered into cells through endocytosis, so the yellow fluorescence in MCF-7 cells was very bright due to abundant 1,5-CDs. While there was little or no LAT1 on the EC surface, only a small amount of 1,5-CDs were bound to LAT1 and entered EC cells through endocytosis and EC cells had a low fluorescence intensity [56].

Fig. 8c and d showed the effect of CDs binding to different amounts of phenylalanine on cell imaging during the same incubation time. With the increase of surface amino groups in CDs, phenylalanine reacted with CDs increased, the amounts of CDs entering cells also increased correspondingly, and the intracellular fluorescence became stronger. This phenomenon suggested that an increase in the number of amino groups provided more binding sites for phenylalanine, allowing more CDs to enter cells. However, there was no amino group on the surface of 0-CDs, and there was only a small amount of phenylalanine on the surface of 1-CDs, so the number of entering MCF-7 cells was small. Therefore, in the laser confocal microscope image, the fluorescence in the cells was not very strong.

We also incubated MCF-7 cells first with phenylalanine solution for 24 h, and then with 1,5-CDs solution, and found that the fluorescence intensity in the cells was also weak (Fig. 8e). Because phenylalanine is a high-affinity substrate of LAT1, after culturing phenylalanine with cells, LAT1 binds to phenylalanine in the cells, and the amount of unbound LAT1 decreases, resulting in a reduced ability of cancer cells to transport 1,5-CDs. The number of 1,5-CDs entering the MCF-7 cells decreased.
The above research showed that 1,5-CDs had the ability to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells.

In conclusion, by a one-step solvothermal method, phenylalanine-bound CDs were prepared with yellow fluorescence and dual fluorescence emission peak behavior, of which one emission peak was excitation-dependent and the other was not. In addition, surface groups such as amino and carboxyl groups had a certain effect on the fluorescence of CDs. When we removed the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, the fluorescence intensity decreased. The prepared CDs had solvent effect, and the emission peaks of CDs shifted due to the comprehensive factors such as the polarity of different solvents. Based on this, we developed a method for the detection of water content in ethanol using 1,5-CDs as a probe. The detection linearity was good. The obtained CDs had good biocompatibility and could be transported into cancer cells by LAT1 on the surface of cancer cells, while normal cells only had a small amount of CDs, so 1,5-CDs had been used by us to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. The prepared CDs could be used for biological imaging, laying a foundation for the preparation of cancer diagnostic materials.

Hao Zou: Conceptualization, Date curation, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Xiaokun Liao: Investigation. Xingjian Lu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaoyan Hu: Investigation. Yue Xiong: Investigation. Jun Cao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jiaqi Pan: Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chaorong Li: Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yingying Zheng: Funding acquisition, Writing - review and editing, Methodology, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51802282) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114746.
